column4: Systems for collecting and storing rainfall from roofs, courtyards, slopes, or catchments for household, agricultural, and municipal use.
Earthen, stone, or masonry barriers and storage basins that impound water for irrigation, flood control, urban supply, and power.
Gently sloping underground galleries with vertical shafts that tap aquifers and deliver groundwater by gravity to settlements and fields.
Water-flushed sanitation fixture using a trap seal to isolate indoor spaces from sewer gases while moving waste into drains.
Dedicated sewer pipe networks that carry human wastewater away from buildings for discharge or treatment separately from stormwater.
Elevated storage tanks and pressure-managed mains that stabilize municipal water delivery and firefighting supply.
Biologically active sand-bed filtration method that removes turbidity and pathogens from municipal drinking water.
High-rate granular filtration process using coagulation, sedimentation, and backwashing for large municipal waterworks.
Chemical dosing and mixing process that destabilizes fine particles so they aggregate and settle or filter out of water.
On-site wastewater systems that settle solids and discharge clarified effluent to soil absorption fields for decentralized sanitation.
Laboratory and field methods for measuring microbial, chemical, and physical water quality to guide treatment and regulation.
Urban drains, culverts, detention basins, and channels designed to collect runoff and reduce street flooding.
Water disinfection and oxidation process using ozone gas to inactivate pathogens and degrade taste, odor, or micropollutants.
Disinfection process using UV light reactors to inactivate microorganisms without adding chemical residuals.
Water treatment process using exchange media to remove hardness ions or selected contaminants from water.
Public water treatment practice of adjusting fluoride concentration to reduce dental caries at population scale.
Microfiltration, ultrafiltration, nanofiltration, and related membrane processes for removing particles, pathogens, and dissolved contaminants.
Pressure exchangers, turbines, and process designs that recover energy in reverse-osmosis desalination plants.
Wastewater treatment systems that combine biological treatment with membrane separation to produce high-quality effluent.
Treatment trains and distribution controls that convert municipal or industrial wastewater into water for irrigation, industry, groundwater recharge, or potable reuse.
Processes that generate highly reactive oxidants such as hydroxyl radicals to degrade trace organic contaminants in water.
Activated carbon, ion exchange, high-pressure membrane, and destruction-oriented treatment approaches for PFAS-contaminated water streams.
Supervisory control and data acquisition systems used to monitor pumps, tanks, valves, treatment plants, and distribution networks.
Sensors and correlation methods that locate water-main leaks by analyzing pipe noise and pressure transients.
Networked sensors, meters, analytics, and control systems for managing leakage, pressure, water quality, and demand in utility networks.
Forecast membrane and process systems aiming to lower desalination energy, fouling, brine, and cost barriers for water-scarce regions.
